checklist.query: The marriage-rating regressor barely seems to earn its place. Couldn't we just drop rateMar from the spec entirely? How much does the fit actually lean on it?
Age looks like it could be redundant once years-married is in the model. Couldn't we leave the Age term out altogether? What's it really contributing to the fit?
We're evaluating a leaner specification that excludes the years-married regressor. With YearsMa held out of the fit, what total absolute deviation does the censored estimate settle at?
There's a proposal to run this as a no-intercept model — force the constant term to zero. What does the fit's total absolute deviation become without an intercept?

manual.query: Couldn't we just run every reaction at the full 60-unit batch and keep operations uniform? What would that simple full-batch rule give us?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('RTN_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: run each reaction at its full 60-unit batch (at its scheduled firing)
model.E['Rx1', 5].fix(60)
model.E['Rx2', 4].fix(60)
model.E['Rx3', 3].fix(60)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: run every reaction at the full 60-unit batch'
new_version = 'RTN_mip__heur_full_batch'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Couldn't we just keep it simple and run all the reactions on a single reactor — leave Reactor_2 idle the whole time? What net value would that give us?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('STN_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: leave Reactor_2 idle - run everything on Reactor_1
for k in model.W:
    if k[1] == 'Reactor_2':
        model.W[k].fix(0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: leave Reactor_2 idle, run all reactions on Reactor_1'
new_version = 'STN_mip__heur_no_reactor2'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: A simple desk rule: just book half of the available downside — set the negative part to half the magnitude of the floor and call it done. How far off the model's answer is that?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('absmip_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'half-the-floor': recognize a negative part equal to half the magnitude of
# the lower bound (computed from xlo), staying on the negative branch (b=0).
xn_val = abs(value(model.xlo)) / 2.0
model.xn.fix(xn_val)
model.b.fix(0)
description = 'Heuristic: set xn to half the |lower bound| on the negative branch'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'absmip_mip__heur_half', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['absmip_mip__heur_half']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Good land is the best ground we've got, so shouldn't we just work it the hardest first — commit the whole good-land block up front to our top cash crop, sugar-cane, fill all 8.775 hectares with it, and let the solver sort out the medium land, the labor and the herd around that? Wouldn't planting the prime ground to our most valuable crop already hold a competitive farm income?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('agreste_lp293', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic land-priority rule: commit the prime (good) land class first, to a single
# high-value crop. crop-10 is the sugar-cane cropping activity (a[crop-10]=1). Dedicate
# all good-land cropping to crop-10, saturate the 8.775 ha good block, then solve the
# residual (medium land + labor + livestock + sales).
for p in model.p:
    if p != 'crop-10':
        model.xcrop[p, 'good'].fix(0)
model.good_land_priority = Constraint(expr=model.xcrop['crop-10', 'good'] == model.landc['good'])
new_version = 'agreste_lp293__heuristic_good_land_cane'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: dedicate the whole good-land block to sugar-cane (crop-10), saturate 8.775 ha, solve residual'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: We always say work the best ground hardest first — good land is our scarcest, most valuable class. Crop-10 is already the activity the plan trusts most on that ground, so shouldn't we just commit the entire good-land block to crop-10 up front, lock everything else off the good land, and let the solver fill in medium and pasture around that? Dedicating our prime acreage to the one crop that earns it ought to beat the optimizer's scattered good-land mix.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('agreste_lp297', models_dictionary)
# Land-priority heuristic: commit the prime (good) land class to a single dedicated
# crop (crop-10, the activity the plan already trusts most there), then solve the residual.
for (p, s) in model.ps:
    if s == 'good' and p != 'crop-10':
        model.xcrop[p, 'good'].fix(0)
new_version = 'agreste_lp297__heuristic_good_land_crop10'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: dedicate the entire good-land class to crop-10 up front; solver sizes medium/pasture residual'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Couldn't we just spread our ten type-a jets evenly — two on every route — instead of dumping them all on one? What would that simple split cost us?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('aircraft_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: spread the type-a fleet evenly, 2 aircraft on each route
for j in model.j:
    model.x['a', j].fix(2)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: even split of type-a fleet, 2 aircraft per route'
new_version = 'aircraft_lp__heur_spread_a'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:

manual.query: Operations is pushing back on the current fleet mix — they want the deployed small-capacity types (a and d) across the network to be at least as many airframes as the deployed large-capacity types (b and c) combined, otherwise we're too exposed if a wide-body goes down for maintenance. Run the numbers under that constraint and tell me what total cost looks like.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('airsp2_lp134', models_dictionary)
model.fleet_mix = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x['a', j] for j in model.j) + sum(model.x['d', j] for j in model.j)
       >= sum(model.x['b', j] for j in model.j) + sum(model.x['c', j] for j in model.j)
)
description = 'What-if: deployed (a+d) >= deployed (b+c) across the network'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'airsp2_lp134__fleet_mix_small_geq_large', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: These mixed-type route assignments make crewing and ground handling a headache. Wouldn't a clean tiered plan run smoother — put the small-capacity types a and d only on the short runs route-1 and route-2, and keep the wide-bodies b and c on the trunk routes 3, 4 and 5 — and just let the solver settle the exact counts inside that layout? Shouldn't that tidier structure still hold a competitive cost?

Our chief ops officer is worried we lean too hard on the large-capacity fleet — she wants the small-capacity types (a and d) combined deployment across the network to be at least as many airframes as the large-capacity types (b and c) combined. What does that restriction do to total operating cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('airsp2_lp87', models_dictionary)
model.fleet_mix = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x['a', j] for j in model.j) + sum(model.x['d', j] for j in model.j)
       >= sum(model.x['b', j] for j in model.j) + sum(model.x['c', j] for j in model.j)
)
description = 'What-if: deployed (a+d) >= deployed (b+c) across the network'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'airsp2_lp87__fleet_mix_small_geq_large', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: All this spreading every aircraft type thin across five routes feels like it just multiplies the operating headaches. Wouldn't a clean home-route plan run smoother — make type-a the home fleet for route-1, type-b for routes 3 and 4, and type-c for routes 2 and 5 — assign each route one home aircraft type up front, keep every other type off it, and let the solver settle the exact counts and soak up whatever's left as bumping? Shouldn't that tidy dedicated layout still hold a competitive cost?

Our compliance officer just flagged that we lean heavily on type-b and type-c — regulators want to see the small-fleet types pulling their weight. The new rule: total deployed type-a-and-d aircraft across the network has to be at least as large as total deployed type-b-and-c. What does this do to expected cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('airsp_lp121', models_dictionary)
model.fleet_balance = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x['a', j] for j in model.j if ('a', j) in model.ij)
       + sum(model.x['d', j] for j in model.j if ('d', j) in model.ij)
       >= sum(model.x['b', j] for j in model.j if ('b', j) in model.ij)
       + sum(model.x['c', j] for j in model.j if ('c', j) in model.ij)
)
description = 'What-if: regulator rule — total deployed small-fleet (a+d) >= total deployed large-fleet (b+c)'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'airsp_lp121__fleet_balance_small_geq_large', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: There's a proposal to guarantee a minimum presence on the long-haul run for crew-rostering reasons — keep at least 28 aircraft (any type) deployed on route-5. If we put that floor in, where does expected cost land?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('airsp_lp121', models_dictionary)
model.route5_min = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x[i, 'route-5'] for i in model.i if (i, 'route-5') in model.ij) >= 28
)
description = 'What-if: at least 28 aircraft deployed on route-5'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'airsp_lp121__route5_min28', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Wouldn't a route-5-only strategy beat the current diversified plan? Route-5 has the lowest c[i,j]/p[i,j] ratio across all aircraft types here, so it's clearly the cheapest-per-passenger run. Pin x[i, route-5] = aa[i] for every type i and let the solver fill in bumping. Shouldn't this concentrated plan outperform the current diversified allocation?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('airsp_lp121', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a cheapest-per-seat route-dedication plan set in advance, then solve the residual.
# For every aircraft type, route-5 minimizes c[i,j]/p[i,j], so dedicate each type's full
# availability to route-5 and let the solver settle the bumping.
for i in model.i:
    model.x[i, 'route-5'].fix(value(model.aa[i]))
    for j in model.j:
        if (i, j) in model.ij and j != 'route-5':
            model.x[i, j].fix(0)
new_version = 'airsp_lp121__heuristic_route5_dedication'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: dedicate every aircraft type fully to route-5 (lowest c/p ratio); solver fills in bumping'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Our compliance officer keeps pushing back on type-c concentration — they want type-c usage capped so it doesn't exceed 1.5× total type-d usage on the network. How does that hit expected cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('airsp_lp165', models_dictionary)
model.type_c_cap = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x['c', j] for j in model.j if ('c', j) in model.ij)
       <= 1.5 * sum(model.x['d', j] for j in model.j if ('d', j) in model.ij)
)
description = 'What-if: cap total deployed type-c at 1.5x total deployed type-d'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'airsp_lp165__type_c_le_1p5_type_d', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: All these mixed-type route assignments make crewing and ground handling a nightmare. Wouldn't a clean dedication plan run smoother — assign each aircraft type to its single cheapest route by operating cost and pour the whole sub-fleet onto that one run, then let the solver settle the bumping? Shouldn't that tidy each-type-to-one-route layout still hold a competitive cost?

Plant management's been pushing back that machine-1 keeps running underutilized while machine-3 is hammered. They want machine-1's running hours locked at no less than 85% of machine-3's running hours from now on, however the shift schedule shakes out. How does that hit the profit?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('ajax_lp', models_dictionary)
model.run_balance = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.outp[g, 'machine-1'] / model.prate[g, 'machine-1'] for g in model.g)
       >= 0.85 * sum(model.outp[g, 'machine-3'] / model.prate[g, 'machine-3'] for g in model.g)
)
description = 'What-if: machine-1 running hours >= 0.85 * machine-3 running hours'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'ajax_lp__run_balance_m1_geq_85pct_m3', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Grade changeovers on these machines eat hours we never recover. Wouldn't a clean one-grade-per-line plan run smoother — dedicate machine-1 to 20-bond-wt, machine-2 to 25-bond-wt, and let machine-3 carry both c-bond-ext and tissue-wrp — and just let the solver settle the exact tonnages from there? Shouldn't that specialized layout still hold a competitive profit?

Do we really need the solver here? Our ops lead just loads the bigger cannery first — fill San Diego with its cheapest runs up to capacity, then let Seattle mop up the rest. How much does that simple rule actually cost us?

One of the old dispatchers swears by cross-hauling to keep both plants busy — northern plant takes the southern orders and the southern plant takes the northern ones. Seattle runs Topeka, San Diego runs Chicago, and they split New York. What does that habit really cost compared to the plan?

Honestly, can't we skip the search and just hand the solver the digit assignment a human puzzler would write down — G=8, E=4, O=6, R=5, I=0, A=2, N=3, V=1, M=9, T=7 — and let it confirm the carries? If we nail down every letter to that guess, how does the carry total compare with what the optimizer found on its own?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('alphamet_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'hand-solved guess': fix every letter to the digit a human solver would write,
# then let the model confirm the carries fall out and the equation still balances.
guess = {'g': 8, 'e': 4, 'o': 6, 'r': 5, 'i': 0, 'a': 2, 'n': 3, 'v': 1, 'm': 9, 't': 7}
jlist = list(model.j)
for ltr, dg in guess.items():
    model.x[ltr, jlist[dg]].fix(1)
description = 'Heuristic: fix every letter to the hand-solved digit guess'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'alphamet_mip__heur_guess', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['alphamet_mip__heur_guess']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our planners like a blunt capital rule: only ever invest in expanding the mines that sit on the biggest known bauxite reserves, and freeze expansion everywhere else. If we lock the model to expanding only the eleven largest-reserve deposits, how much worse is the total cost than the optimizer's own pick of where to invest?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('alum_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'expand-the-richest-mines': permit a fixed-segment mine expansion ONLY at the K
# deposits holding the largest proven bauxite reserve (zmbar), and freeze the 0-1 expansion
# flag to zero at every other mine. K = 11, the number the optimizer itself chose to expand.
res = {i: value(model.zmbar[i]) for i in model.i}
K = 11
keep = set(sorted(model.i, key=lambda i: -res[i])[:K])
for i in model.i:
    if i not in keep:
        model.ym[i].fix(0)
description = 'Heuristic: allow mine expansion only at the 11 largest-reserve deposits'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'alum_mip__heur_richest', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['alum_mip__heur_richest']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Sales is locking down a long-running channel partner — the deal hinges on us guaranteeing at least 10 nuts produced across the four-period horizon. We're evaluating that commitment; plug the floor in and tell me what the optimizer returns for profit.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('ampl_lp', models_dictionary)
model.nuts_floor = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x['nuts', t] for t in model.t) >= 10
)
description = 'What-if: sales contract floor on total nuts across the horizon (>=10)'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'ampl_lp__nuts_floor_10', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: There's a proposal on the table to commit to a washers volume for a distributor — at least 20 washers across the whole horizon. We're costing it out; if we hold total washers to 20 or more, what does the optimizer return for profit?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('ampl_lp', models_dictionary)
model.washers_floor = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x['washers', t] for t in model.t) >= 20
)
description = 'What-if: distributor commitment floor on total washers across the horizon (>=20)'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'ampl_lp__washers_floor_20', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: We're evaluating a warm-start rule that keeps the lines busy from day one: produce at least 30 units total in period 1 rather than letting the plan idle the opening period. With that period-1 floor in place, where does profit land?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('ampl_lp', models_dictionary)
model.period1_floor = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x[p, 1] for p in model.p) >= 30
)
description = 'What-if: warm-start floor of 30 total units produced in period 1'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'ampl_lp__period1_floor_30', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Switching products mid-period costs us changeover time we never get back. Wouldn't a clean one-product-per-period campaign run smoother — dedicate period 1 to nuts, period 2 to bolts, period 3 to washers, and period 4 back to nuts — and just let the solver settle the exact tonnages from there? Shouldn't that disciplined campaign still hold a competitive profit?

Honestly the current schedule looks chaotic to the planners. Why not run a clean one-product-per-period rotation — nuts in period 1, bolts in period 2, washers in period 3, nuts again in period 4, a steady 8-unit batch each time, well inside what the raw stock can carry? Shouldn't that tidy rotation still hold its own on profit?
Some on the council argue for a fully inward-looking plan — produce only for the regional market and export nothing at all. How badly does that no-export stance hurt the bottom line?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('andean_fixed', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: autarky -- no exports of anything
for idx in model.e:
    model.e[idx].fix(0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: no exports at all (autarky)'
new_version = 'andean_fixed__heur_no_exports'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: What if we treated urea as strictly a domestic product and exported none of it region-wide, leaning on the other fertilizers for export earnings? Where does the plan's cost land under that rule?
What if we don't bother optimizing the switches at all and just leave every binary off — let the continuous parts do all the work? How far off the optimum is that lazy configuration?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('asyncincbi_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'all-off': switch every binary off and let the free continuous xlo/xhi
# absorb the coupling residual; the bottleneck xmax is then driven entirely by rhs.
for j in model.bins:
    model.b[j].fix(0)
description = 'Heuristic: leave every binary switched off'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'asyncincbi_mip__heur_alloff', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['asyncincbi_mip__heur_alloff']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Cost-cutting just dropped from corporate — they want our combined monthly shipments capped at 800 cases going forward, down from the 900 we ship today. Plug that in and tell me where the cost lands.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('asyncloop_lp', models_dictionary)
model.total_shipment_cap = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.i for j in model.j) <= 800
)
description = 'What-if: corporate-mandated cap on combined monthly shipments (<=800 cases)'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'asyncloop_lp__total_cap_800', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Seattle's running flat out at 100% utilization while San Diego's only at 92%. Couldn't we require both plants run within 5 percentage points of each other on capacity utilization? Wouldn't load-balancing keep us competitive on cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('asyncloop_lp', models_dictionary)
# Utilization = total shipments from plant / plant capacity (a is fixed at base values).
_util = {i: sum(model.x[i, j] for j in model.j) / value(model.a[i]) for i in model.i}
model.util_balance_upper = Constraint(expr=_util['seattle'] - _util['san-diego'] <= 0.05)
model.util_balance_lower = Constraint(expr=_util['seattle'] - _util['san-diego'] >= -0.05)
description = 'Why-not: tie the two plants utilization rates within 5 percentage points of each other'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'asyncloop_lp__util_balance_5pct', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Our ops lead keeps pushing a load-balanced shipping plan — spread each market's order across the plants in proportion to nameplate capacity, so Seattle takes its 350/950 share of every market and San Diego takes its 600/950 share. Wouldn't that even split come out ahead of the current lopsided one on freight cost? What's the model seeing that pushes it the other way?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('asyncloop_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a capacity-proportional split set in advance, then solve the residual.
# Every market's order is dealt to the two plants in proportion to their nameplate
# capacities (Seattle 350/950, San Diego 600/950); the solver settles z from there.
total_cap = sum(value(model.a[i]) for i in model.i)
for i in model.i:
    share = value(model.a[i]) / total_cap
    for j in model.j:
        model.x[i, j].fix(share * value(model.b[j]))
new_version = 'asyncloop_lp__heuristic_proportional_share'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: pin x[i,j] = (a[i]/sum_a) * b[j] (capacity-proportional split)'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: This isn't a real tour — it's just a pile of little loops, cities bouncing back and forth between paired partners. Shouldn't we ban those reciprocal back-and-forth pairs outright? What's the model seeing that makes it lean on them?

Cities 2, 3 and 4 just curl into their own sealed three-city loop, cut off from everything else. Wouldn't it be better to break that closed triangle so they connect into the wider plan? What's the trade-off keeping them sealed together?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('awktsp_mip_assign', models_dictionary)
S = ['i2', 'i3', 'i4']
model.break_tri = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[a, b] for a in S for b in S if a != b) <= 2)
description = 'Why-not: break the 2-3-4 triangle loop'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'awktsp_mip_assign__break_tri', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Why run an optimizer at all — couldn't we just send everything round in one big loop, city 1 to 2 to 3 and on through 7 and back to 1? Wouldn't that simple round-trip be good enough?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('awktsp_mip_assign', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: one big numbered-order loop
route = ['i1', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i5', 'i6', 'i7']
for a, b in zip(route, route[1:] + route[:1]):
    model.x[a, b].fix(1)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: one big numbered-order loop'
new_version = 'awktsp_mip_assign__heur_bigloop'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Couldn't we just eyeball a sensible loop — run 1 over to its cheap partner 5, then through the 2-3-4 group, and finish via 6 and 7 before heading home? Wouldn't that hand-drawn route be good enough?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('awktsp_mip_assign', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: hand-drawn loop 1-5-2-3-4-6-7
route = ['i1', 'i5', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i6', 'i7']
for a, b in zip(route, route[1:] + route[:1]):
    model.x[a, b].fix(1)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: hand-drawn loop 1-5-2-3-4-6-7'
new_version = 'awktsp_mip_assign__heur_handdrawn'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: This isn't a real tour — it's just a pile of little loops, cities bouncing back and forth between paired partners. Shouldn't we ban those reciprocal back-and-forth pairs outright? What's the model seeing that makes it lean on them?

Cities 2, 3 and 4 just curl into their own sealed three-city loop, cut off from everything else. Wouldn't it be better to break that closed triangle so they connect into the wider plan? What's the trade-off keeping them sealed together?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('awktsp_mip_cut', models_dictionary)
S = ['i2', 'i3', 'i4']
model.break_tri = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[a, b] for a in S for b in S if a != b) <= 2)
description = 'Why-not: break the 2-3-4 triangle loop'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'awktsp_mip_cut__break_tri', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Why run an optimizer at all — couldn't we just send everything round in one big loop, city 1 to 2 to 3 and on through 7 and back to 1? Wouldn't that simple round-trip be good enough?

route = ['i1', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i5', 'i6', 'i7']
for a, b in zip(route, route[1:] + route[:1]):

manual.query: Our planners like a no-fuss rule: give every delivery point its own dedicated line straight from the depot — open one direct n1-to-sink arc per customer and ship each its order on that. If we lock the network into that hub-and-spoke pattern, how much more does the build cost than the optimizer's relayed tree?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('bchfcnet_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'direct hub-and-spoke': give every demand node its own dedicated arc
# straight from the source n1, and open no other arc. Sink set computed from model.demand.
sinks = [j for j in model.n if value(model.demand[j]) > 0]
direct = set(('n1', j, 'a1') for j in sinks)
for (m_, n_, sa) in model.arc:
    model.y[m_, n_, sa].fix(1 if (m_, n_, sa) in direct else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: dedicated direct n1 -> sink arc per demand node (hub-and-spoke)'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'bchfcnet_mip__heur_direct', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['bchfcnet_mip__heur_direct']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our investment committee likes a dead-simple rule: grab the most valuable column first, then keep adding the next-most-valuable one as long as it still fits every resource budget. If we lock the program to that greedy most-valuable-first pick, how much value do we leave on the table versus the optimizer's plan?

The schedulers would much rather keep it simple: one dedicated pattern per product, each roll cut into just a single width, no fiddly mixed layouts. If we ran the line that way — only the single-width patterns p1 through p4 and none of the combination cuts — how many more rolls would it cost us versus the optimizer's mixed plan?

Our dispatchers don't trust the fancy routing — they'd rather just run the obvious greedy rule: start at the first city and always drive to the nearest stop you haven't hit yet, then loop back home. If we lock the route to that nearest-neighbor pattern, how much longer is the trip than the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('bchtsp_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'nearest-neighbour': start at the first city; at each step drive to the
# cheapest not-yet-visited city (ties broken by city label, deterministic), then close
# the loop back to the start. Fix the resulting arc pattern and let the solver price it.
cities = list(model.city)
start = cities[0]
route = [start]
unvisited = set(cities) - {start}
cur = start
while unvisited:
    nxt = min(unvisited, key=lambda j: (value(model.c[cur, j]), j))
    route.append(nxt)
    unvisited.discard(nxt)
    cur = nxt
for a in range(len(route)):
    i = route[a]
    j = route[(a + 1) % len(route)]
    for k in model.city:
        if k != i:
            model.x[i, k].fix(1 if k == j else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: nearest-neighbour tour from the first city'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'bchtsp_mip__heur_nn', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['bchtsp_mip__heur_nn']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Couldn't we just give every vendor their entry-level deal — take each supplier's first-segment bid — and be done with it? What would that simple even-handed split cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('bid_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: award every vendor its first-segment (entry-level) deal
for v in model.v:
    if (v, 1) in model.vs:
        model.plb[v, 1].fix(1)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: award each vendor its first-segment deal'
new_version = 'bid_mip__heur_all_seg1'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Our category managers prefer a dead-simple sourcing rule: rank vendors by their cheapest marginal price and award each one its whole cheapest tier, cheapest vendor first, until the requirement is covered — anyone not needed gets nothing. If we lock the award to that single-tier ladder, how much worse is the procurement bill than the optimizer's blended plan?

Our category managers prefer a dead-simple sourcing principle: for supply-security reasons no vendor should walk away empty-handed — every supplier carries at least some real bid rather than 'nodeal'. If we lock the plan to that no-walk-away rule, how much more does the total purchase bill come to than the optimizer's concentrated plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('bidsos_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'no walk-aways': for supply-security reasons procurement forbids the 'nodeal'
# segment for every vendor, so each supplier must carry a real bid; the optimizer then meets
# the requirement spread across all five vendors instead of concentrating on the cheapest few.
for v in model.v:
    model.pl[v, 'nodeal'].fix(0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: no walk-aways - every vendor takes a real bid'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'bidsos_mip__heur_spread', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['bidsos_mip__heur_spread']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Engineering flagged that items i1 and i2 react badly and can't ride in the same bin. If we enforce that they're always kept apart, does the bin count go up?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('binpacking_mip', models_dictionary)
model.incompatible = ConstraintList()
for j in model.J:
    model.incompatible.add(model.y['i1', j] + model.y['i2', j] <= 1)
description = 'What-if: items i1 and i2 may not share a bin'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'binpacking_mip__i1_i2_separate', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: The optimizer's assignment looks like it's scattering items all over the place. These items practically come in natural groups — couldn't we just drop the first three into bin 1, the next three into bin 2, and so on for the opening stretch, then let the solver clean up whatever's left? Wouldn't that simple sequential fill get us there without costing any extra bins?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('binpacking_mip', models_dictionary)
items = list(model.I)
bins = list(model.J)
# Heuristic: seed the first ten bins by dropping consecutive items three at a time,
# then let the solver pack whatever is left.
for k in range(10):
    for i in items[3 * k:3 * k + 3]:
        model.y[i, bins[k]].fix(1)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: fill the first ten bins sequentially (three consecutive items each) and solve the rest'
new_version = 'binpacking_mip__heur_sequential_fill'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Vendor onboarding and QC are a headache for the premium alloys, so we'd rather just deal with our four lowest-priced suppliers — alloys a, b, c and d — and let the optimizer settle the exact split among those four. Shouldn't sticking to the value tier still hit the spec at a competitive cost? What's keeping that restricted-sourcing plan from being the cheapest?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('blend_lp54', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a value-tier sourcing plan set in advance, then solve the residual.
# Restrict procurement to the four lowest-priced alloys (a, b, c, d) and let the
# solver settle the split; forbid every premium alloy (e..i).
value_tier = {'a', 'b', 'c', 'd'}
for a in model.alloy:
    if a not in value_tier:
        model.v[a].fix(0.0)
new_version = 'blend_lp54__heur_value_tier_abcd'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: dedicate sourcing to the four cheapest alloys a,b,c,d; forbid premium alloys; solve residual'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Our purchasing folks would rather not juggle nine different alloy suppliers. Their instinct is to dedicate the whole blend to the three lead-rich workhorses we already qualify in volume — a, b and d — and just let the proportions settle from there. Shouldn't a tidy three-supplier plan like that hold up on cost?

The plan stows all fourteen of the smallest cartons, but our handlers find them fiddly. Couldn't we just leave at least five of those little ones behind and not lose much? Cap the small-carton count at nine and tell me what it actually costs us in volume.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('boxpacking_mip', models_dictionary)
small = ['b17','b18','b19','b20','b21','b22','b23','b24','b25','b26','b27','b28','b29','b30']
model.few_small = Constraint(expr=sum(model.OMEGA[b] for b in small) <= 9)
description = 'Why-not: keep at most 9 of the 14 small cartons'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'boxpacking_mip__few_small', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Our loaders prefer a no-brainer rule: only bother stowing the bigger cartons and leave every one of the smallest half-size boxes off this run. If we lock the load to that 'mid-and-large only' rule, how much packed volume do we give up versus the optimizer's pack-everything plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('boxpacking_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'mid-and-large only': stow every carton at or above the mid volume class
# (>= 0.24624 m3) and leave the smallest half-size cartons (0.11825 m3) on the dock.
thresh = 0.24624
for b in model.b:
    if value(model.box_vol[b]) < thresh - 1e-9:
        model.OMEGA[b].fix(0)
    else:
        model.OMEGA[b].fix(1)
description = 'Heuristic: stow only cartons at or above the mid volume class'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'boxpacking_mip__heur_big', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['boxpacking_mip__heur_big']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our planners want a dead-simple sourcing rule: hand each region to a single warehouse — the cheapest-haul one that still has room for that region's full order, taking the biggest regions first. If we lock the network to that one-region-one-warehouse pattern, how much more does the freight-plus-fixed bill come to than the optimizer's mixed plan?

Our purchasing team would rather lean on the two cheapest grains and let the solver patch the rest. Couldn't we anchor the blend on a fixed base of 40% barley and 20% oats up front, then fill in whatever sesame and ground meal it takes to hit the spec? Shouldn't that cheap-staples-first plan stay close to the current cost — what's keeping the model from leaning that way on its own?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('chance_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a cheap-staples-first base plan set in advance, then solve the residual.
# Commit the two cheapest grains (barley, oats) to a fixed 40/20 base, then let the
# solver fill in sesame + ground meal to close the protein/fat gap.
base = {'barley': 0.40, 'oats': 0.20}
for f, frac in base.items():
    model.x[f].fix(frac)
new_version = 'chance_lp__heur_cheap_staples_first'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: anchor on cheap grains (barley 0.40, oats 0.20) up front, solve residual'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: The county is rolling out a soil-rotation rule next cycle — no single cropping sequence should exceed 30% of the paddy land planted in any cycle. How does that anti-monoculture rule shift brigade income and the sequence mix?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('china_lp', models_dictionary)
total_xcrop = sum(model.xcrop[s, f] for (s, f) in model.ss)
seqs = sorted(set(s for (s, _) in model.ss))
model.diversification_cap = ConstraintList()
for s in seqs:
    seq_total = sum(model.xcrop[s_idx, f_idx] for (s_idx, f_idx) in model.ss if s_idx == s)
    model.diversification_cap.add(seq_total <= 0.30 * total_xcrop)
new_version = 'china_lp__seq_cap_30'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, 'each sequence capped at 30% of total paddy planted')
print('obj=', models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])

manual.query: There's a proposal to keep the high-fertilizer rotations from dominating the paddy — hold all the high-fertilizer sequences together to no more than 40% of the paddy planted each cycle. What does the optimizer return for brigade income and the plan under that ceiling?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('china_lp', models_dictionary)
total_xcrop = sum(model.xcrop[s, f] for (s, f) in model.ss)
high_total = sum(model.xcrop[s, f] for (s, f) in model.ss if f == 'high')
model.high_fert_cap = Constraint(expr=high_total <= 0.40 * total_xcrop)
new_version = 'china_lp__highfert_cap_40'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, 'all high-fertilizer sequences <= 40% of total paddy planted')
print('obj=', models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])

manual.query: Upland is a small sliver of the brigade's land — and the plan still keeps a tight ratio between fodder and vegetable. Couldn't we require that no upland crop ever exceed 60% of total upland area? What's the model seeing that I'm not?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('china_lp', models_dictionary)
total_upland = sum(model.xupland[c] for c in model.cu)
model.upland_cap = ConstraintList()
for c in model.cu:
    model.upland_cap.add(model.xupland[c] <= 0.60 * total_upland)
new_version = 'china_lp__upland_cap_60'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, 'why-not: cap each upland crop at 60% of total upland')
print('obj=', models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])

manual.query: The plan barely touches barley, yet barley is our hardiest grain and the cadres want it kept in the rotation. Shouldn't the barley sequences carry at least a quarter of the paddy we plant? What's keeping the model from leaning on barley like that?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('china_lp', models_dictionary)
total_xcrop = sum(model.xcrop[s, f] for (s, f) in model.ss)
barley_total = sum(model.xcrop[s, f] for (s, f) in model.ss if s.startswith('bar'))
model.barley_floor = Constraint(expr=barley_total >= 0.25 * total_xcrop)
new_version = 'china_lp__barley_floor_25'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, 'why-not: barley sequences carry at least 25% of total paddy planted')
print('obj=', models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])

manual.query: Green-manure-rice-rice on high fertilizer is taking a huge slice of the paddy on its own. Couldn't we hold any single high-fertilizer sequence to no more than 20% of the paddy planted so the plan isn't betting the cycle on one rotation? What's the model seeing that makes it concentrate like that?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('china_lp', models_dictionary)
total_xcrop = sum(model.xcrop[s, f] for (s, f) in model.ss)
model.high_seq_cap = ConstraintList()
for (s, f) in model.ss:
    if f == 'high':
        model.high_seq_cap.add(model.xcrop[s, f] <= 0.20 * total_xcrop)
new_version = 'china_lp__high_seq_cap_20'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, 'why-not: each high-fertilizer sequence capped at 20% of total paddy planted')
print('obj=', models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])

manual.query: Chopping and changing the fertilizer regime on each rotation makes the field-work plan a mess. Wouldn't it be cleaner to just dedicate each rotation to one fertility band up front — run the barley and wheat sequences on high fertilizer and keep the green-manure, rape and fallow sequences on normal — and let the solver settle the exact mu from there? What's the tradeoff the model's flagging that I'm not seeing?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('china_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a fixed fertility-band dedication plan set in advance, then solve the residual mu.
# barley & wheat sequences -> high fertilizer; green-manure, rape & fallow sequences -> normal.
def dedicated_band(s):
    return 'high' if (s.startswith('bar') or s.startswith('wh')) else 'normal'
for (s, f) in model.ss:
    if f != dedicated_band(s):
        model.xcrop[s, f].fix(0.0)
new_version = 'china_lp__heur_band_dedication'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, 'heuristic: dedicate bar/wh sequences to high fertilizer and gm/rape/fallow to normal, solve residual')
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
obj = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'] if status == 'optimal' else None
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj))

manual.query: Some analysts would just skip the fancy estimator and report a null model — set every regression coefficient to zero so the prediction is the same censored constant for everyone, and let the residuals fall where they may. If we lock the fit to that no-covariates rule, how much worse is the total absolute deviation than the full CLAD estimate?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('clad_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'null model': ignore every regressor by fixing all beta coefficients to zero,
# so each household's fitted value is driven purely by the censoring disjunction (not by X).
# The slack/surplus vars then absorb the residuals optimally on the fixed-coefficient model.
for pp in model.p:
    model.beta[pp].fix(0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: null model — all regression coefficients fixed to zero'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'clad_mip__heur_null', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['clad_mip__heur_null']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Do we need to optimize the tranche selection at all? A desk could just take the first three normal tranches in order — n1, n2, n3 — and call it a day. How much does that lazy pick cost in proceeds?
What if we just throw every normal tranche into the structure — all six, kitchen-sink style? Where does that leave the proceeds?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('cmo_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: include all six normal tranches
for n in model.N:
    model.tin[n].fix(1)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: include all six normal tranches'
new_version = 'cmo_mip__heur_all6'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Suppose the black army has to spread out one-per-rank for visibility — no more than a single black queen in any given row. With that dispersion rule in place, what's the biggest each army can be?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('coex_mip', models_dictionary)
model.b_one_per_row = ConstraintList()
for r in model.i:
    model.b_one_per_row.add(sum(model.b[r, j] for j in model.i) <= 1)
description = 'What-if: at most one black queen per row'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'coex_mip__b_one_per_row', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Our planners like a dead-simple territorial rule: keep the two armies completely off each other's turf by banding them — black only in the top two rows, white only in the bottom two rows — and let the solver fill those bands. How many queens per side does that tidy banded layout actually buy us versus the optimizer's nine?

Our investment committee likes a blunt rule: just build out the richest deposits. Take the five locations with the largest total ore reserves and green-light a mine, smelter and refinery expansion at every one of them, then let the solver size and route the rest. How much more does that 'build at the richest deposits' plan cost than the optimizer's selective build?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('copper_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'build at the richest deposits': rank locations by total (high+med) ore reserves,
# force a full open-pit/smelter/refinery expansion at the top five, let the solver do the rest.
tot = {i: value(model.reserves[i, 'high-grade']) + value(model.reserves[i, 'med-grade']) for i in model.i}
ranked = sorted(model.i, key=lambda i: -tot[i])
build = set(ranked[:5])
for i in build:
    for mm in model.mm:
        model.ym[mm, i].fix(1)
description = 'Heuristic: force expansion at the five largest-reserve locations'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'copper_mip__heur_richest', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['copper_mip__heur_richest']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: There's a proposal on the table to standardize the output so the template matches reference string s2 exactly at every position. If we lock it to s2's pattern, what worst-case distance does that leave us with?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('csp_mip', models_dictionary)
s2_pattern = {'c1': 'a13', 'c2': 'a5', 'c3': 'a4', 'c4': 'a9', 'c5': 'a1', 'c6': 'a14'}
model.lock_s2 = ConstraintList()
for mi, ai in s2_pattern.items():
    model.lock_s2.add(model.v[mi, ai] == 1)
description = 'What-if: lock the template to match reference string s2 at every position'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'csp_mip__lock_to_s2', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: The optimizer builds a brand-new template character by character, but we already have four perfectly good strings on hand. Couldn't we just reuse one of them — say s1 — as the template instead of synthesizing a new one? What's the model seeing that makes the custom template worth it?

seed = [('a', j, k) for j in labels for k in labels]
extra = [('b', 'a', 'a'), ('b', 'a', 'b'), ('b', 'a', 'c'), ('b', 'b', 'a')]

manual.query: Couldn't we just pack a solid quarter-block — fill the whole 2-by-2 column running up one edge and cap it off with a single far cell? Wouldn't a compact chunk like that be simpler than the spread-out optimal layout?

Our floor crew would rather keep it simple: give each finished width its own dedicated pattern — the one that yields the most of that width — and just run that pattern enough times to cover its order, never mixing widths across patterns. If we lock the schedule to that one-pattern-per-product rule, how much worse is the roll count than the optimizer's mixed plan?
manual.constraint:
import math
model = load_model('cutstock_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'one dedicated pattern per width': for each finished width pick the single
# pattern that yields the most of it, then run that pattern ceil(demand/yield) times.
# A pattern claimed by more than one width takes the largest of the implied run counts.
best = {}
for i in model.i:
    cand = [(value(model.aip[i, p]), p) for p in model.p if value(model.aip[i, p]) > 0]
    y, p = max(cand)
    best[i] = (p, y)
runs = {p: 0 for p in model.p}
for i in model.i:
    p, y = best[i]
    runs[p] = max(runs[p], math.ceil(value(model.d[i]) / y))
for p in model.p:
    model.xp[p].fix(runs[p])
description = 'Heuristic: one dedicated (highest-yield) pattern per finished width'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'cutstock_mip__heur_dedicated', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['cutstock_mip__heur_dedicated']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our planners like a no-frills loading rule: take the customers heaviest-order-first and drop each into the first truck that still has room, then let routing fill in the driving. If we lock the fleet to that first-fit-by-size assignment, how much worse is the total distance than the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('cvrp_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'first-fit-decreasing': sort customers by demand (largest first) and assign
# each to the first vehicle that still has remaining capacity; then force each customer
# to be entered only by its assigned vehicle and let the solver order the stops.
custs = [j for j in model.node if j not in model.depot]
custs_sorted = sorted(custs, key=lambda j: -value(model.demand[j]))
veh = list(model.vehicle)
rem = {k: value(model.capacity[k]) for k in veh}
assign = {}
for j in custs_sorted:
    d = value(model.demand[j])
    for k in veh:
        if rem[k] >= d:
            assign[j] = k
            rem[k] -= d
            break
model.heur = ConstraintList()
for j in custs:
    for k in veh:
        rhs = 1 if assign[j] == k else 0
        model.heur.add(sum(model.X[i, j, k] for i in model.node if i != j) == rhs)
description = 'Heuristic: first-fit-decreasing customer-to-vehicle assignment'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'cvrp_mip__heur_ffd', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['cvrp_mip__heur_ffd']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Right now plant-2 is doing all the heavy lifting on terminal 2 while plant-1 sends nothing there. Couldn't we require both plants to carry at least 25% of every terminal's demand? Wouldn't that more balanced sourcing leave us with a reasonable shipping bill?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('decomp_lp97', models_dictionary)
model.both_min25 = ConstraintList()
for i in model.i:
    for j in model.j:
        model.both_min25.add(model.x[i, j] >= 0.25 * model.b[j])
description = 'Why-not: force every plant to cover at least 25 percent of every terminal demand'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'decomp_lp97__wn_both_min25', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Operations is uncomfortable with how concentrated the volume is on a single lane — plant-2 to terminal 2 alone is carrying nearly half the network's flow. Couldn't we require that no individual plant-terminal route carry more than 40% of the network's total throughput? What's the tradeoff that's pulling the model toward such a lopsided plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('decomp_lp97', models_dictionary)
model.route_cap_40 = ConstraintList()
for i in model.i:
    for j in model.j:
        model.route_cap_40.add(model.x[i, j] <= 0.40 * sum(model.x[ip, jp] for ip in model.i for jp in model.j))
description = 'Why-not: cap any single lane at 40 percent of total network throughput'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'decomp_lp97__wn_route_cap_40', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: The plant-2-to-terminal-2 lane still dominates even with a loose cap. Couldn't we tighten the rule so no single route exceeds 30% of the network's total throughput? I'd like to understand what that extra diversification really costs us.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('decomp_lp97', models_dictionary)
model.route_cap_30 = ConstraintList()
for i in model.i:
    for j in model.j:
        model.route_cap_30.add(model.x[i, j] <= 0.30 * sum(model.x[ip, jp] for ip in model.i for jp in model.j))
description = 'Why-not: cap any single lane at 30 percent of total network throughput'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'decomp_lp97__wn_route_cap_30', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Couldn't we just run a simple primary-carrier rule instead of this lane-by-lane optimization? Make plant-1 our lead carrier — have it fill terminals 1 and 2 to their full requirement first, since those are the ones it picks up on the way out, then let plant-2 mop up whatever's left at terminals 3 and 4. Wouldn't that kind of straightforward dispatch rule give us a perfectly workable plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('decomp_lp97', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic planning strategy: plant-1 is the primary carrier. Following a fixed
# dispatch sequence, it fully serves terminals 1 and 2 first (exhausting its run),
# and is taken off terminals 3 and 4; plant-2 covers the residual demand there.
model.heur_primary = ConstraintList()
model.heur_primary.add(model.x['plant-1', 'term-1'] == model.b['term-1'])
model.heur_primary.add(model.x['plant-1', 'term-2'] == model.b['term-2'])
model.x['plant-1', 'term-3'].fix(0.0)
model.x['plant-1', 'term-4'].fix(0.0)
new_version = 'decomp_lp97__heur_primary_carrier'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, 'heuristic: plant-1 primary-carrier dispatch on terminals 1 and 2, plant-2 covers the rest')
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
obj = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'] if status == 'optimal' else None
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj))

manual.query: The cooperative is rolling out a labor-equity rule next season — temporary labor in any single month can't exceed 40% of the total labor used that month. Plug that fairness constraint in and tell me where farm income and the cropping mix land.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('demo1_lp', models_dictionary)
model.temp_cap_40 = ConstraintList()
for t in model.t:
    model.temp_cap_40.add(model.tlab[t] <= 0.40 * (model.flab[t] + model.tlab[t]))
description = 'What-if: cooperative labor-equity rule caps temp labor at 40 percent of monthly total'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'demo1_lp__temp_cap_40', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Hiring out family labor in the slack months feels wasteful when we're still pulling in temporary workers during peak. Couldn't we require that the family hours we lend out in any given month never exceed the temp labor we bring in that same month? What's pulling the plan toward this hire-out-while-hiring-in pattern?

model = load_model('demo1_lp', models_dictionary)
model.own_labor_first = ConstraintList()
for t in model.t:

description = 'Why-not: forbid family-labor hire-out in any month from exceeding temp hires that month'

manual.query: Cotton, onions, and tomato carry the fattest margins on this farm by a mile. Why not just pre-allocate by that pecking order — hand cotton its two hectares first, give onions and tomato a hectare each, and skip the low-margin crops entirely? Wouldn't that margin-first plan come out ahead of the model's fussy spread?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('demo1_lp', models_dictionary)
priority_plan = {'cotton': 2.0, 'onions': 1.0, 'tomato': 1.0}
for c in model.c:
    model.xcrop[c].fix(priority_plan.get(c, 0.0))

manual.query: Honestly, why not just hand each die a clean block of consecutive numbers — die1 gets 1 through 6, die2 gets 7 through 12, die3 gets 13 through 18? That's the obvious way to lay out three dice. Lock the faces to that block layout and tell me how it stacks up against the optimizer's win count.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('dice_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'consecutive blocks': give each die a contiguous run of integers
# (die1 = flo..flo+5, die2 = flo+6..flo+11, die3 = flo+12..flo+17), then let the
# solver count the resulting wins on the fixed face layout.
flo = int(value(model.flo))
faces = list(model.f)
dlist = list(model.dice)
n = len(faces)
for di, d in enumerate(dlist):
    for fi, f in enumerate(faces):
        model.fval[d, f].fix(flo + di * n + fi)
description = 'Heuristic: consecutive integer blocks per die'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'dice_mip__heur_block', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['dice_mip__heur_block']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: A simpler rule of thumb: take the numbers 1 through 18 in order and just deal them round-robin onto the three dice, like dealing cards. Die1 gets 1,4,7,10,13,16; die2 gets 2,5,8,11,14,17; die3 gets 3,6,9,12,15,18. If we fix the faces to that dealt pattern, how many wins per die does it manage versus the optimizer?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('dice_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'round-robin deal': sort flo..flo+17 and deal cyclically onto the dice,
# so each die gets every third value, then count the wins on that fixed layout.
flo = int(value(model.flo))
faces = list(model.f)
dlist = list(model.dice)
nd = len(dlist)
nf = len(faces)
allvals = list(range(flo, flo + nd * nf))
for di, d in enumerate(dlist):
    col = [allvals[di + nd * fi] for fi in range(nf)]
    for fi, f in enumerate(faces):
        model.fval[d, f].fix(col[fi])
description = 'Heuristic: round-robin deal of 1..18 onto the three dice'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'dice_mip__heur_interleave', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['dice_mip__heur_interleave']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Skip the heavy grid search for a second — why not just give each die a straight block of consecutive numbers, die1 with 1 to 6, die2 with 7 to 12, die3 with 13 to 18? That's the most natural partition. Lock the faces into that block layout and tell me how it compares to the optimizer's win count.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('dicegrid_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'consecutive blocks': give each die a contiguous run of integers
# (die1 = flo..flo+5, die2 = flo+6..flo+11, die3 = flo+12..flo+17), then let the
# solver count the resulting wins on the fixed face layout.
flo = int(value(model.flo))
faces = list(model.f)
dlist = list(model.dice)
n = len(faces)
for di, d in enumerate(dlist):
    for fi, f in enumerate(faces):
        model.fval[d, f].fix(flo + di * n + fi)
description = 'Heuristic: consecutive integer blocks per die'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'dicegrid_mip__heur_block', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['dicegrid_mip__heur_block']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Here's a dead-simple alternative to the parallel search: take 1 through 18 in order and deal them round-robin across the three dice, like dealing a deck. Die1 gets 1,4,7,10,13,16; die2 gets 2,5,8,11,14,17; die3 gets 3,6,9,12,15,18. Fix the faces to that dealt pattern — how many wins per die does it land versus the full optimization?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('dicegrid_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'round-robin deal': sort flo..flo+17 and deal cyclically onto the dice,
# so each die gets every third value, then count the wins on that fixed layout.
flo = int(value(model.flo))
faces = list(model.f)
dlist = list(model.dice)
nd = len(dlist)
nf = len(faces)
allvals = list(range(flo, flo + nd * nf))
for di, d in enumerate(dlist):
    col = [allvals[di + nd * fi] for fi in range(nf)]
    for fi, f in enumerate(faces):
        model.fval[d, f].fix(col[fi])
description = 'Heuristic: round-robin deal of 1..18 onto the three dice'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'dicegrid_mip__heur_interleave', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['dicegrid_mip__heur_interleave']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our product team likes a dead-simple rule for laying out the pips: sort the eighteen values and deal them out round-robin, so each die gets every third value (die 1 the smallest of each triple, die 2 the middle, die 3 the largest), then sort each die's six faces. If we lock the dice to that striped pattern, how many wins per die does it actually deliver versus the optimizer's tailored design?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('dicex_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'striped deal': sort the 18-value pool, deal it round-robin across the
# three dice (die k gets pool[k::3]), then sort each die's faces ascending and fix
# the face values to that pattern; let the solver count the resulting wins.
dlist = list(model.d)
flist = list(model.f)
nd = len(dlist)
pool = sorted(int(round(value(model.fnum[d, f]))) for d in model.d for f in model.f)
for di, d in enumerate(dlist):
    vals = sorted(pool[di::nd])
    for fi, f in enumerate(flist):
        if not (d == dlist[0] and f == flist[0]):
            model.fval[d, f].fix(vals[fi])
description = 'Heuristic: striped round-robin face-value deal'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'dicex_mip__heur_stripe', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['dicex_mip__heur_stripe']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why bother optimizing the whole export schedule sector by sector? Couldn't we just ship every merchandize line at its minimum legal export floor and be done with it — how much does that cost us on initial consumption?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('dinam_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic export policy: run every merchandize commodity at its lower export bound
# (elo) in every optimization year instead of optimizing the export mix.
for r in model.im:
    for y in model.t:
        model.e[r, y].fix(value(model.elo[r, y]))
description = 'Heuristic: ship every merchandize line at its minimum export floor (elo)'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'dinam_lp__heur_export_floor', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['dinam_lp__heur_export_floor']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why run the whole optimization? Couldn't we just plant the high-value perennials and cash crops — cotton, citrus and sugarcane — flat out to their regional land ceilings everywhere they're allowed, and let the rest fall where it may?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('egypt_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic cash-crop policy: plant the high-value perennials/cash crops (cotton,
# citrus, sugarcane) up to each region's land ceiling (their xcrop upper bounds,
# which come from the upbnds table), leaving everything else to the solver.
perennials = ['cotton', 'citrus', 'sugarcane']
for r in model.r:
    for c in perennials:
        if (r, c) in model.xcrop:
            ub = model.xcrop[r, c].ub
            if ub is not None:
                model.xcrop[r, c].fix(ub)
description = 'Heuristic: max out cotton/citrus/sugarcane to regional ceilings'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'egypt_lp__heur_perennial_max', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['egypt_lp__heur_perennial_max']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Here's a dead-simple dispatch rule: lean on our biggest brewery first. Push every hub's keg order through San Diego until it's tapped out, and only spill the leftover onto Seattle. Wouldn't running the big house flat-out before touching the small one already hold a competitive distribution cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('embmiex1_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic big-hub-first policy: fill the largest brewery (by capacity) across every
# hub up to its limit, then send only the overflow to the smaller brewery.
big = max(model.i, key=lambda i: value(model.a[i]))
small = min(model.i, key=lambda i: value(model.a[i]))
rem = value(model.a[big])
for j in model.j:
    take = min(rem, value(model.b[j]))
    model.x[big, j].fix(take)
    model.x[small, j].fix(value(model.b[j]) - take)
    rem -= take
description = 'Heuristic: route every hub through the bigger brewery first, overflow to the smaller'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'embmiex1_lp__heur_big_hub', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['embmiex1_lp__heur_big_hub']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why agonize over the dispatch at all? Couldn't we just lean green — run the renewables flat out first, then backfill whatever's left with the cheapest conventional units in order?

Our planners would rather skip the solver and use a simple rule of thumb: rank items by their combined primary-plus-secondary value per unit of resource, grab them top-down while both budgets hold, and if the secondary floor isn't met yet, swap in the highest secondary-value leftovers until it is. If we lock the basket to that greedy recipe, how far does the primary score fall short of the optimizer's plan?

Sugar beets are grabbing 60% of the field — that's a soil-health red flag if I've ever seen one. Couldn't we require that no single crop's acreage exceed half the total cultivated land? Walk me through what's pulling the optimizer toward such a concentrated rotation.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('farm_lp86', models_dictionary)
model.crop_diversity_50 = ConstraintList()
for c in model.crop:
    model.crop_diversity_50.add(model.x[c] <= 0.50 * sum(model.x[cc] for cc in model.crop))
description = 'Why-not: cap any single crop area at 50 percent of total cultivated acreage'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'farm_lp86__wn_crop_diversity_50', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: The whole field is leaning on sugar beets while wheat and corn sit on token plots. Shouldn't every crop be carrying real ground — say at least 100 acres each — so we're not one bad beet season away from disaster? What's keeping the model from spreading the acreage out like that?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('farm_lp86', models_dictionary)
model.acreage_floor = ConstraintList()
for c in model.crop:
    model.acreage_floor.add(model.x[c] >= 100)
description = 'Why-not: every crop gets at least 100 acres of ground'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'farm_lp86__wn_acreage_floor_100', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Sugar beets are clearly the moneymaker per acre, so couldn't we run the field on a dead-simple priority rule: plant just enough wheat and corn to clear the feed minimums and pour every remaining acre into sugar beets? Lock that allocation in and let the solver settle the sell-and-buy side. Shouldn't that beat whatever the model is doing — what's it seeing that pushes it off the beets?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('farm_lp86', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a fixed priority plan set in advance, then solve the residual.
# Cover each feed minimum with the least acreage, push every remaining acre into sugar beets.
wheat_acres = pyo.value(model.minreq['wheat']) / pyo.value(model.yield_param['wheat'])
corn_acres = pyo.value(model.minreq['corn']) / pyo.value(model.yield_param['corn'])
beets_acres = pyo.value(model.land) - wheat_acres - corn_acres
model.x['wheat'].fix(wheat_acres)
model.x['corn'].fix(corn_acres)
model.x['sugarbeets'].fix(beets_acres)
new_version = 'farm_lp86__heur_sugarbeets_priority'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: cover feed minimums with least acreage, push remaining acres into sugarbeets; solve residual'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Trading desk wants to de-risk crude concentration — they're floating a sourcing rule that no single crude should ever exceed 50% of total crude purchased on a given cycle. How does that diversification mandate shift profit and the crude mix?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('fawley_lp', models_dictionary)
model.crude_diversity_50 = ConstraintList()
for c in model.cr:
    model.crude_diversity_50.add(model.u[c] <= 0.50 * sum(model.u[cc] for cc in model.cr))
description = 'What-if: cap any single crude purchase at 50 percent of total crude'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'fawley_lp__crude_diversity_50', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Pipestill is sitting around 90% utilization and reformer's at 89% while c-cracker's pegged at the wall. Couldn't we require that every productive unit run at least 95% of its nameplate every cycle to squeeze more value out of the existing kit? What's keeping the model from pushing them harder?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('fawley_lp', models_dictionary)
model.min_util_95 = ConstraintList()
for k in model.k:
    model.min_util_95.add(model.cap[k] >= 0.95 * model.kp[k])
description = 'Why-not: require capacity utilization on every productive unit to stay at or above 95 percent of nameplate'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'fawley_lp__wn_min_util_95', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Our blenders keep asking why we juggle three different recipes for the distillate products when a simpler standing playbook would be easier to run. Couldn't we just dedicate jet-fuel to recipe-3 and run all heat-oil on recipe-1, shut the other recipe slots, and let the rest of the plant settle around that fixed blending plan? Wouldn't that streamlined recipe discipline hold the margin?
Why grind through an optimizer for routing? Couldn't we just send each plant's whole output to its single cheapest market on the freight sheet and let the relaxation cover whatever's left short?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('feasopt1_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic single-source policy: send each plant's entire capacity to the one market
# where its per-case transport cost c[i,j] is lowest, ignoring how much that market
# actually needs. Over-shipment to a small market is wasted and other markets go
# unserved, so the demand-relaxation variables must cover the rest.
for i in model.i:
    cheapest = min(model.j, key=lambda j: value(model.c[i, j]))
    for j in model.j:
        model.x[i, j].fix(value(model.a[i]) if j == cheapest else 0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: each plant ships entirely to its lowest-cost market'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'feasopt1_lp__heur_cheapest', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['feasopt1_lp__heur_cheapest']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Treasury would love a blunt self-reliance rule: in every period, don't let imported final products exceed a small slice of that period's total nutrient demand, so domestic plants carry the load. If we hold imports to at most 6.5 percent of each period's combined nitrogen-plus-phosphate demand, how much more does the program cost than the optimizer's free-import plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('fertd_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'self-reliance ceiling': in each period cap total final-product imports at
# 6.5% of that period's combined nutrient demand (n + p2o5, summed from db over regions),
# forcing domestic plants to shoulder more of the load than the optimizer's free-import plan.
model.imp_cap = ConstraintList()
for t in model.t:
    dtot = sum(value(model.db[cq, j, t]) for cq in model.cq for j in model.j)
    model.imp_cap.add(sum(model.vf[c, j, tt] for (c, j, tt) in model.vf if tt == t) <= 0.065 * dtot)
description = 'Heuristic: cap each period final-product imports at 6.5% of that period nutrient demand'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'fertd_mip__heur_selfreliance', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['fertd_mip__heur_selfreliance']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why agonize over which superphosphate plant runs flat out? The three SSP plants have the same capacity — couldn't we just split total SSP-155 production evenly across them and call it a balanced plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('ferts_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic balanced-load policy: rather than let the optimizer pick which SSP plant
# runs flat-out, split total SSP-155 output evenly across the plants that actually have
# SSP capacity (read from k), sized to total SSP-155 demand (read from cf75).
ssp_plants = [i for i in model.i if value(model.k['ssp', i]) > 0]
total_ssp = sum(value(model.cf75[j, 'ssp-155']) for j in model.j)
share = total_ssp / len(ssp_plants)
for i in ssp_plants:
    model.z['ssp-155', i].fix(share)
description = 'Heuristic: even SSP-155 production split across the capacitated plants'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'ferts_lp__heur_even_ssp', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['ferts_lp__heur_even_ssp']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Do we really need to optimize the whole opening from scratch? Let's just commit the leaper to a known good launch sequence out of the corner — leap (1,1) across to (1,6), swing down to (4,2), cut over to (8,5), then climb to (4,8) — and let it improvise the rest. Does locking in that four-leap opening still leave it a full closed tour?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('fiveleap_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: commit a fixed four-leap opening chain out of the corner, optimize the rest
model.xm['1', '1', '1', '6'].fix(1)
model.xm['1', '6', '4', '2'].fix(1)
model.xm['4', '2', '8', '5'].fix(1)
model.xm['8', '5', '4', '8'].fix(1)
