manual.query: Our plant operators would much rather run one fixed recipe all year than re-tune the blend every month. Take the three oils whose hardness sits closest to the middle of the allowed [3,6] window and lock the blend to exactly those same three oils every month. How much profit do we give up versus the optimizer's month-by-month mix?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('food_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'single fixed recipe': pick the maxnusep oils whose hardness is closest to the
# midpoint of the allowed hardness window (hmin+hmax)/2 -- a recipe guaranteed to be able to
# hit spec -- and run that same blend every month, letting the solver set the volumes.
target = (value(model.hmin) + value(model.hmax)) / 2.0
k = int(value(model.maxnusep))
keep = set(sorted(model.p, key=lambda pp: abs(value(model.h[pp]) - target))[:k])
for mm in model.m:
    for pp in model.p:
        model.induse[mm, pp].fix(1 if pp in keep else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: single mid-hardness fixed recipe held every month'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'food_mip__heur_fixed_recipe', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['food_mip__heur_fixed_recipe']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our planners want a no-brainer dispatch rule instead of the solver's bespoke plan: take the bulkiest items first — the ones that eat the most capacity wherever they go — and drop each onto whichever resource can still hold it at the cheapest placement cost. If we lock the assignment to that greedy rule, how much more does the total cost run versus the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('gapmin_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'heaviest-first cheapest-feasible': order items by descending minimum usage (the
# bulkiest jobs first), and place each on the resource whose remaining capacity still admits it
# at the lowest assignment cost f[i,j]; then lock the assignment and let the solver price it.
rem = {i: value(model.b[i]) for i in model.i}
order = sorted(model.j, key=lambda j: -min(value(model.a[i, j]) for i in model.i))
assign = {}
for j in order:
    cands = [i for i in model.i if rem[i] >= value(model.a[i, j])]
    pick = min(cands, key=lambda i: value(model.f[i, j]))
    assign[j] = pick
    rem[pick] -= value(model.a[pick, j])
for i in model.i:
    for j in model.j:
        model.x[i, j].fix(1 if assign[j] == i else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: heaviest-first cheapest-feasible greedy assignment'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'gapmin_mip__heur_greedy', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['gapmin_mip__heur_greedy']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Honestly, the obvious move is just to split every market between the two plants in proportion to their capacity — Seattle is 350 out of 950 total cases, San Diego is 600 out of 950, so each market draws 350/950 from Seattle and 600/950 from San Diego. Wouldn't that even-handed proportional split come in cheaper than the lopsided routing the model picked?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('gussex1_lp', models_dictionary)
# Capacity-proportional heuristic: assign in advance a uniform sourcing rule where each
# market is split between the two plants in the ratio of their capacities, then solve the
# residual. Seattle carries 350/950 of every market, San Diego carries 600/950.
seattle_share = 350.0 / 950.0
sd_share = 600.0 / 950.0
for j in model.j:
    bj = float(value(model.b[j]))
    model.x['seattle', j].fix(seattle_share * bj)
    model.x['san-diego', j].fix(sd_share * bj)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: each market split between plants in proportion to capacity (350/950 from Seattle, 600/950 from San Diego)'
new_version = 'gussex1_lp__capacity_proportional_heuristic'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Why not keep dispatch dead simple — load up our Seattle plant first, run its whole output across the lines before we even touch San Diego, and let San Diego just mop up whatever's left? Wouldn't filling the near plant before the far one hold a competitive logistics cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('gussgrid_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic small-hub-first policy: exhaust the smaller plant (by capacity) across the
# assembly lines first, then send only the overflow to the larger plant.
small = min(model.i, key=lambda i: value(model.a[i]))
big = max(model.i, key=lambda i: value(model.a[i]))
rem = value(model.a[small])
for j in model.j:
    take = min(rem, value(model.b[j]))
    model.x[small, j].fix(take)
    model.x[big, j].fix(value(model.b[j]) - take)
    rem -= take
description = 'Heuristic: exhaust the smaller plant first, overflow to the larger'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'gussgrid_lp__heur_small_hub', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['gussgrid_lp__heur_small_hub']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Looking at the approximation, consecutive node values jump around more than I'd expect for a smooth physical curve. Shouldn't we require neighboring approximation values to differ by no more than 0.25? What's keeping the model from picking a smoother fit?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('imsl_lp', models_dictionary)
m_list = list(model.m)
def _smooth_up(mm, idx, _ml=m_list):
    if idx == len(_ml) - 1:
        return Constraint.Skip
    return mm.ym[_ml[idx + 1]] - mm.ym[_ml[idx]] <= 0.25
def _smooth_dn(mm, idx, _ml=m_list):
    if idx == len(_ml) - 1:
        return Constraint.Skip
    return mm.ym[_ml[idx + 1]] - mm.ym[_ml[idx]] >= -0.25
model.smooth_up = Constraint(range(len(m_list)), rule=_smooth_up)
model.smooth_dn = Constraint(range(len(m_list)), rule=_smooth_dn)
description = 'Why-not: bound the change between consecutive approximation nodes to 0.25 to enforce smoothness'
new_version = 'imsl_lp__smoothness_cap_025'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

The physics gives us three landmarks for free — the signal starts at 0, peaks at 1, and returns to 0 — so couldn't we just pin those three anchor nodes a-00, a-05 and a-10 to that known geometry and let the fit sort out only the in-between nodes? Wouldn't that landmark-first plan be good enough?

# Heuristic plan: pin the three landmark nodes to the known sine geometry
# (start=0, peak=1, return=0) up front, then fit only the interior nodes.
landmarks = {'a-00': 0.0, 'a-05': 1.0, 'a-10': 0.0}
for k, val in landmarks.items():
    model.ym[k].fix(val)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: pin landmark nodes ym[a-00]=0, ym[a-05]=1, ym[a-10]=0 and fit the interior'
new_version = 'imsl_lp__heur_landmark_pin'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

else:

manual.query: Do we really need to grind through the full optimization? Couldn't we just pick our ten highest-value activities and agree up front to run them at no more than half their combined capacity, and let the rest of the plan fill in around that?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('indus89_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'half-the-headline-capacity': take the ten activities with the largest
# positive surplus coefficients that carry a finite upper bound, and pre-commit their
# COMBINED activity to at most half of their summed capacity (computed from the data),
# then let the optimizer balance everything else.
cand = sorted(
    [v for v in model.V if value(model.objc[v]) > 0 and model.x[v].ub is not None],
    key=lambda v: -value(model.objc[v]),
)[:10]
budget = 0.5 * sum(value(model.x[v].ub) for v in cand)
model.heur_topcap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[v] for v in cand) <= budget)
description = 'Heuristic: cap the 10 top-surplus capped activities at half their combined ub'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'indus89_lp__heur_topcap', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['indus89_lp__heur_topcap']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Couldn't we keep procurement simple and just buy whichever raw material is cheapest per ton, split evenly across the two periods up to our storage limit? Chase the lowest sticker price on the input and let outsourcing cover the gaps. Wouldn't that hold a competitive cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('kand_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic cheapest-input policy: buy ONLY the raw material with the lowest unit cost c
# (ignoring its yield), split evenly across the periods up to the inventory cap.
cheapest = min(model.i, key=lambda i: value(model.c[i]))
per_period = value(model.b) / len(model.t)
for i in model.i:
    for t in model.t:
        model.x[i, t].fix(per_period if i == cheapest else 0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: source only the cheapest-by-unit-cost raw material, even split across periods'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'kand_lp__heur_cheapest_input', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['kand_lp__heur_cheapest_input']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our pickers don't want to run an optimizer every cycle — they'd rather use a back-of-the-envelope rule: rank items by bang-for-the-buck (profit per unit weight) and grab them in that order until the next one won't fit. If we pack the bag that greedy way, how much profit do we give up versus the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('knapsack_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic greedy-by-density: rank items by profit-to-weight ratio and take each, in
# that order, while the remaining capacity still admits it; then lock the selection.
items = sorted(model.i, key=lambda i: value(model.p[i]) / value(model.w[i]), reverse=True)
rem = value(model.c)
chosen = set()
for i in items:
    wi = value(model.w[i])
    if wi <= rem:
        chosen.add(i)
        rem -= wi
for i in model.i:
    model.x[i].fix(1 if i in chosen else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: greedy fill by profit/weight ratio'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'knapsack_mip__heur_greedy', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['knapsack_mip__heur_greedy']['obj']['value']))

Here's a simple recipe for the first square: step the value forward by three each column instead of one, row over row. Slap that pattern down and let the other square fall in around it — does it still work out?

Operations wants a contingency rule: take the single highest-value line out of service — the one whose endpoints together could serve the most direct demand — and let the rest of the network cover the edge requirements around it. If we sideline that one busiest line, how much direct ridership do we give up versus the optimizer's full plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('lop_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'sideline the busiest line': score every candidate line by the total OD demand it
# could carry directly (both endpoints sit on the line), pick the single highest-scoring line,
# and take it out of service (frequency 0). The remaining lines must still meet every edge's
# frequency requirement; the solver re-sets frequencies on the surviving lines.
_line_has_station = set((la, lb, st) for (la, lb, st) in model.rp_idx)
score = {}
for ln in model.line:
    tot = 0.0
    for (o, d) in model.odpair:
        if (ln[0], ln[1], o) in _line_has_station and (ln[0], ln[1], d) in _line_has_station:
            tot += value(model.od[o, d])
    score[ln] = tot
busiest = max(model.line, key=lambda l: score[l])
model.sideline = Constraint(expr=model.phi[busiest] <= 0)
description = 'Heuristic: take the highest-demand line out of service'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'lop_mip__heur_sideline', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['lop_mip__heur_sideline']['obj']['value']))

Our puzzle-desk has a quick rule of thumb: the two most-connected hub circles are the easiest to keep separated from everything if you nail them to the two extreme digits, so pin the busiest circle to 1 and the next-busiest to 8 and let the rest fall out. If we lock the layout to that hubs-take-the-extremes rule, does the digit total move off what the full optimizer finds?
manual.constraint:
from collections import defaultdict
model = load_model('marilyn_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'hubs take the extremes': rank circles by adjacency degree and pin the two
# most-connected hub circles to the extreme digits 1 and 8 (a high-degree circle is
# easiest to keep >=gap from all its neighbors at an extreme value); the solver fills
# the remaining circles around those two anchors.
deg = defaultdict(int)
for (c, cc) in model.net:
    deg[c] += 1
hubs = sorted(model.c, key=lambda c: (-deg[c], c))[:2]
model.x[hubs[0]].fix(1)
model.x[hubs[1]].fix(8)
description = 'Heuristic: pin the two hub circles to the extreme digits 1 and 8'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'marilyn_mip__heur_hub', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['marilyn_mip__heur_hub']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Disruption hedging would seem to argue for keeping a real buffer at the top. Couldn't we require that at least 15% of the total steady-state mass sits on the 21-billion-barrel action? Wouldn't that fuller-reserve posture still leave us reasonably close to today's expected cost?

model = load_model('markov_lp', models_dictionary)
T = sum(model.z[s, i, sp] for s in model.s for i in model.i for sp in model.sp)
model.full_floor = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.z[s, i, 21] for s in model.s for i in model.i) >= 0.15 * T)
description = 'Why-not: at least 15% of mass sits at the 21-level action'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'markov_lp__full_floor15', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: It looks like the policy leans on running the reserve all the way down to empty as a cheap action. Couldn't we hold the steady-state mass landing on the empty action to at most 5% of the total, so we're not constantly emptying out? Wouldn't a less drain-happy plan still come in close to today's expected cost?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('markov_lp', models_dictionary)
T = sum(model.z[s, i, sp] for s in model.s for i in model.i for sp in model.sp)
model.empty_action_cap = Constraint(
    expr=sum(model.z[s, i, 'empty'] for s in model.s for i in model.i) <= 0.05 * T)
description = 'Why-not: drawdown-to-empty action mass <= 5% of total'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'markov_lp__empty_action_cap5', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: All these tiny mid-level shuffles look like operational noise. Couldn't we just run a clean ladder plan — in a normal market only ever hold the current level or step it up by one, and in a disrupted market always hold flat — and let the solver settle the exact mass from there? Wouldn't that disciplined ladder come out close on expected cost?

Instead of a full optimization, our operators want a dead-simple rule: walk the nodes in order and drop each one onto whichever side gives the bigger immediate gain against the neighbors already placed. If we lock the partition to that one-pass greedy assignment, how much cut weight do we leave on the table versus the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('maxcut_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'greedy one-pass': visit nodes in index order; place each on whichever side
# maximizes the net weight of cut edges to its already-placed neighbors, then fix x.
import collections
adj = collections.defaultdict(list)
for (i, j) in model.e:
    wij = value(model.w[i, j])
    adj[i].append((j, wij)); adj[j].append((i, wij))
side = {}
for nd in model.n:
    gain_if_1 = sum(w for (nb, w) in adj[nd] if nb in side and side[nb] == 0)
    gain_if_0 = sum(w for (nb, w) in adj[nd] if nb in side and side[nb] == 1)
    side[nd] = 1 if gain_if_1 >= gain_if_0 else 0
for nd in model.n:
    model.x[nd].fix(side[nd])
description = 'Heuristic: greedy one-pass node placement'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'maxcut_mip__heur_greedy', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['maxcut_mip__heur_greedy']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Carrying all that opening stock ties up cash. Couldn't we just start the season empty — pin the initial on-hand inventory to zero at every stage and let backlogs catch up? What would that bare-shelves start actually cost us?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('mesc_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: start the season with no on-hand inventory at any stage
for s in model.M0:
    model.i[0, s].fix(0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: pin initial on-hand inventory to zero at every stage'
new_version = 'mesc_lp__heur_zero_initial'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Why agonize over which mill exports what? Couldn't we just hand each mill an export quota in proportion to its steelmaking-furnace capacity and let it fill that however it can?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('mexls_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic export-quota policy: cap each mill's total exports at a share of the
# overall export ceiling etot, proportional to its steel-furnace (open-hearth +
# BOF + electric-arc) capacity.
steel_units = ['openhearth', 'bof', 'elec-arc']
scap = {s: sum(value(model.ks[u, s]) for u in steel_units if (u, s) in model.ks) for s in model.iss}
tot = sum(scap.values())
etot_val = value(model.etot)
model.export_quota = ConstraintList()
for s in model.iss:
    model.export_quota.add(sum(model.e[c, s] for c in model.ce) <= etot_val * scap[s] / tot)
description = 'Heuristic: per-mill export quota proportional to steel-furnace capacity'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'mexls_lp__heur_export_quota', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['mexls_lp__heur_export_quota']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Do we even need this whole expansion program? Suppose we just freeze capital spending entirely — build nothing new and run on the plants we already have plus imports. How much worse does that come out?
Here's a blunt strategy: just concentrate every new unit at Sicartsa and don't expand anywhere else. Pin all the build-out to that one site and tell me what it costs.
Why agonize over the export allocation? Couldn't we just hand each mill an export quota in proportion to its steel-furnace capacity and fill the whole export ceiling that way?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('mexss_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic export-quota policy: give each plant a share of the export ceiling eb
# in proportion to its steel-furnace (open-hearth + BOF + electric-arc) capacity.
steel_units = ['openhearth', 'bof', 'elec-arc']
scap = {i: sum(value(model.k[u, i]) for u in steel_units) for i in model.i}
tot = sum(scap.values())
eb_val = value(model.eb)
for i in model.i:
    model.e['steel', i].fix(eb_val * scap[i] / tot)
description = 'Heuristic: export quota per plant proportional to steel-furnace capacity'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'mexss_lp__heur_export_quota', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['mexss_lp__heur_export_quota']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Mobilizing and demobilizing the fleet twice is what eats us. Couldn't we just run a clean two-bench campaign — take the whole top bench and the whole second bench out at 100%, walk away, and skip the deep stuff entirely? Wouldn't committing to that two-pass plan up front beat all this fractional dithering on profit?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('mine_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic planning strategy: commit in advance to a two-bench campaign — fully
# extract every accessible block on levels 1 and 2 (x=1), and leave levels 3 and 4
# untouched (x=0). Then solve the residual under that fixed depth profile.
for (l, i, j) in model.D:
    if l in (1, 2):
        model.x[l, i, j].fix(1.0)
    else:
        model.x[l, i, j].fix(0.0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: two-bench campaign — extract levels 1 and 2 fully, skip levels 3 and 4'
new_version = 'mine_lp__heur_two_bench'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Our schedulers swear by a build-ahead rule for the finished good: get every AJ8172 run done in the front of the calendar and keep the tail end of the horizon clear as a service buffer. If we lock AJ8172 so none of its production lands in the last three buckets, how much worse does the weighted production total get versus the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('mrp2_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'build AJ8172 ahead': schedulers want the finished good produced in the front
# of the horizon, leaving the final stretch clear. Ban any AJ8172 production setup later
# than (card - 3) periods from the start, computed from the horizon length.
ord_ = {t: i + 1 for i, t in enumerate(model.TT)}
card = len(list(model.TT))
cutoff = card - 3
for t in model.TT:
    if ord_[t] > cutoff:
        model.d['AJ8172', t].fix(0)
description = 'Heuristic: build AJ8172 ahead, no production setups in the final three periods'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'mrp2_mip__heur_build_ahead', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['mrp2_mip__heur_build_ahead']['obj']['value']))

new_version = 'multipleMB_mip__heur_split'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Do we even need to estimate the coefficients? Just keep the anchored cost term and zero out every other weight, then count how many households that bare-bones rule happens to get right. How far off the fitted model is that?

# Heuristic: skip estimation -- zero out every free coefficient (beta[DCOST] stays
# pinned at 1 for scale) and count the resulting sign coincidences.
for p in model.p:

manual.query: What about a dead-simple pairing rule — act-1 and act-2 in fac-1, act-3 and act-4 in fac-2, act-5 on its own in fac-3? How does that tidy little layout score on interaction cost?
Why churn through a full network optimization? Couldn't we just pre-commit each market to take half its volume off its single cheapest inbound lane and let the rest sort itself out?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('netgen_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'half-off-cheapest': pre-commit each market (positive-demand node) to take
# half of its volume off its single cheapest inbound lane (capacity permitting); the
# optimizer balances the remainder.
for n in model.node:
    dem = value(model.demand[n])
    if dem <= 0:
        continue
    in_arcs = [(i, j) for (i, j) in model.arc if j == n]
    if not in_arcs:
        continue
    cheapest = min(in_arcs, key=lambda a: value(model.cost[a]))
    load = min(0.5 * dem, value(model.capacity[cheapest]))
    model.x[cheapest].fix(load)
description = 'Heuristic: serve half of each market off its cheapest inbound lane'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'netgen_lp__heur_halfcheap', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['netgen_lp__heur_halfcheap']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: A planner's rule of thumb here is to diversify away from the first candidate and just commit to the last column in the superstructure. If we lock the build to that rule, how much more expensive is the master than the optimizer's pick?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('nonsharp_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'build-the-last-candidate': commit to the highest-indexed candidate column
# only, switching every other column off.
cols = list(model.col)
for c in model.col:
    model.y[c].fix(1 if c == cols[-1] else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: build only the last candidate column'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'nonsharp_mip__heur_last', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['nonsharp_mip__heur_last']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our mine planners like a blunt rule: dig every pit to the same uniform depth, picking the shallowest common cut-off that still leaves enough rock to cover total demand, and leave everything below that line in the ground. If we lock the plan to that single uniform-depth pattern, how much worse is the discounted net income than the optimizer's tailored schedule?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('openpit_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'uniform extraction depth': cap every pit at the same maximum depth K.
# K = the shallowest common cut-off whose combined volume over all pits still clears
# the horizon's total demand; force every segment below K shut in all pits, then let
# the solver set the extraction volumes on the fixed open pattern.
slist = list(model.s)
total_demand = sum(value(model.demand[t]) for t in model.t)
K = len(slist)
for k in range(1, len(slist) + 1):
    cap = sum(sum(value(model.evo[p, slist[i]]) for i in range(k)) for p in model.p)
    if cap >= total_demand:
        K = k
        break
for p in model.p:
    for i in range(len(slist)):
        if i + 1 > K:
            model.open[p, slist[i]].fix(0)
description = 'Heuristic: uniform extraction-depth cap at the shallowest demand-feasible depth'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'openpit_mip__heur_unifdepth', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['openpit_mip__heur_unifdepth']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: The cabinet wants a smoother investment build-up — total investment in any year should be at least 5% above the previous year's, beyond the existing non-decreasing rule, so the build-out doesn't stagnate mid-plan. How does that minimum-growth floor on investment shift the development plan and the welfare outcome?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('pak_lp', models_dictionary)
te = list(model.te)
def inv_growth_rule(model, i):
    if i < len(te) - 1:
        return model.ti[te[i+1]] >= 1.05 * model.ti[te[i]]
    return Constraint.Skip
model.inv_growth_floor = Constraint(range(len(te)), rule=inv_growth_rule)
description = 'What-if: total investment grows at least 5% year over year'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'pak_lp__inv_growth_5pct', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: The current plan has consumption crawling in the early years and only catching up later. Couldn't we require that consumption `c[te]` grow at least 5% every single year, as a household-living-standards floor? Wouldn't a steadier, more front-loaded consumption path still land close to today's welfare?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('pak_lp', models_dictionary)
te = list(model.te)
def cons_growth_rule(model, i):
    if i < len(te) - 1:
        return model.c[te[i+1]] >= 1.05 * model.c[te[i]]
    return Constraint.Skip
model.cons_growth_floor = Constraint(range(len(te)), rule=cons_growth_rule)
description = 'Why-not: consumption grows at least 5% year over year'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'pak_lp__cons_growth_5pct', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: The plan leans hard on foreign aid early and then lets it taper off. Couldn't we require that net capital inflow `f[t]` in any single year stay within 30% above the horizon average, as a smoothing rule? Wouldn't a more even aid trajectory still come in close to today's welfare?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('pak_lp', models_dictionary)
N = len(list(model.t))
def aid_smooth_rule(model, t):
    return model.f[t] <= 1.3 * (sum(model.f[tt] for tt in model.t) / N)
model.aid_smooth = Constraint(model.t, rule=aid_smooth_rule)
description = 'Why-not: each year of aid stays within 30% above the horizon average'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'pak_lp__aid_smooth_130pct', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: The non-traded sector is where most of the country's productive base sits — couldn't we just commit the entire investment build-out to non-traded production and shut the traded sector out of the capital plan entirely? Set that single-sector dedication up front and let the solver settle the rest. Wouldn't that focused strategy still come out ahead on welfare?
manual.constraint:

# Heuristic: a non-traded-only investment dedication set in advance, then solve the residual.
# Commit every year of capital formation to the non-traded sector; shut the traded sector out.
for te in model.te:
    model.i[te, 'traded'].fix(0)
new_version = 'pak_lp__heuristic_nontraded_only'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: dedicate all investment to non-traded (i[te,traded]=0); solve residual'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

}

manual.query: Do we really need to optimize a whole branching route tree? Couldn't each source just funnel its entire downstream flow through its single nearest gateway port and let the legs cascade from there? Run that simple one-gateway-per-source rule and tell me what mileage it costs us.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('phosdis_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic single-gateway policy: each source must reach all (N-1) other nodes, so push its
# entire first-hop outflow (N-1 units) through its single cheapest-distance outgoing arc and
# send nothing on its other direct legs; let the solver cascade onward from the gateway.
N = len(list(model.nodes))
for s in model.src:
    outs = sorted([(b, value(model.darc[s, b])) for (a, b) in model.arcs if a == s], key=lambda z: z[1])
    gateway = outs[0][0]
    for (a, b) in model.arcs:
        if a == s:
            model.x[s, a, b].fix(float(N - 1) if b == gateway else 0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: route each source through its single cheapest gateway'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'phosdis_lp__heur_gateway', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['phosdis_lp__heur_gateway']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why grind through an optimizer for this? Couldn't we just play it safe and put the whole book into the two lowest-rated government bonds, us-ser-e and us-ser-f, splitting between them in proportion to their after-tax yield?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('port_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic risk-averse policy: hold only the two lowest-rated (safest) government bonds,
# us-ser-e and us-ser-f, splitting the full budget by their after-tax yield.
safe = ['us-ser-e', 'us-ser-f']
atax = {b: value(model.yld[b]) / 100 * (1 - value(model.taxrate[b])) for b in safe}
tot = sum(atax.values())
budget = 10.0
for b in model.b:
    model.investment[b].fix(budget * atax[b] / tot if b in safe else 0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: safe-government split by after-tax yield'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'port_lp__heur_safe_gov', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['port_lp__heur_safe_gov']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Running four grades through these workstations means constant setup churn. Couldn't we just rank the catalog by unit margin and commit the lines to the top two earners — class-4 at $40 and class-2 at $20 — and shelve the two thin grades, class-1 and class-3, then let the solver settle the exact volumes? Wouldn't that lean margin-first plan hold up against the spread-everything-thin mix?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('prodsp_lp', models_dictionary)
# Margin-priority heuristic: rank the catalog by unit margin (class-4=40, class-2=20,
# class-3=18, class-1=12), commit the lines to the top-two earners and shelve the two
# thinnest grades in advance, then solve the residual for the surviving grades.
shelved = ['class-1', 'class-3']  # two lowest-margin grades
for g in shelved:
    model.x[g].fix(0.0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: margin-rank the catalog, run only the top-two grades (class-4, class-2), shelve class-1 + class-3'
new_version = 'prodsp_lp__margin_top2_heuristic'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Why grind through the optimization at all? Couldn't we just weight each stock in proportion to its mean return — load up on the higher-return names and skip the negative ones — and call it a day?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('qp5_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: weight each stock in proportion to its (non-negative) mean return,
# normalized so the weights sum to 1. Negative-mean stocks get zero weight.
pos_mean = {s: max(value(model.mean[s]), 0.0) for s in model.s}
tot = sum(pos_mean.values())
for s in model.s:
    model.x[s].fix(pos_mean[s] / tot)
description = 'Heuristic: weights proportional to non-negative mean return'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'qp5_lp__heur_meanwt', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['qp5_lp__heur_meanwt']['obj']['value']))

Our depot dispatchers like a dead-simple flexibility rule: keep at least two of the small tu1 units stabled overnight at every depot, no matter what, so each yard has a couple of nimble units ready in the morning. If we lock the plan to that rule, how much more does the overnight stabling cost than the optimizer's leaner mix?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('railcirc_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'two-tu1-per-depot flex reserve': insist on at least two small tu1 units stabled
# overnight at every depot, computed as a fixed reserve of 2 per overnight (depot) arc, then
# let the solver size the rest of the fleet around that floor.
on_by_depot = {a[0]: a for a in model.on}
model.flex_reserve = ConstraintList()
for s in model.s:
    a = on_by_depot[s]
    model.flex_reserve.add(model.f['tu1', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] >= 2)
description = 'Heuristic: two tu1 flex-reserve units stabled at every depot'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'railcirc_mip__heur_flex2', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['railcirc_mip__heur_flex2']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our dispatchers like a dead-simple siting rule: figure out the single best cell to put one drop if everybody had to use it, then the second-best such cell, and just open drops on those two. If we lock the two drops to that pair, how much worse is the total walk than the optimizer's mixed plan?

Honestly, do we even need the matching solver? Just run every single ride on path C and let it sort out who serves whom — pin every trip to path C across the board. How much does that one-lane plan cost us on the total?
manual.constraint:

else:

manual.query: Slamming the whole run into the final period strands the line cold for two periods and back-loads all the risk. Wouldn't a gentle ramp on the high grade — 10 units in the first short period, 20 in the second, 30 in the third, nothing held for the residual slot — run smoother and still hold a competitive profit? What's the model trading away by waiting until the end?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('robert_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a fixed ramp-up operating rule set in advance, then solve the residual.
# Ease the high grade up across the short horizon (10 -> 20 -> 30) instead of
# dumping it all into the final period; low/medium stay off; the period-4 residual
# slot carries nothing. Let the solver settle the stock balance from there.
ramp = {1: 10.0, 2: 20.0, 3: 30.0}
for t in model.t:
    model.x['high', t].fix(ramp[t])
    model.x['low', t].fix(0.0)
    model.x['medium', t].fix(0.0)
for p in model.p:
    model.x[p, 4].fix(0.0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: ramp-up plan on the high grade (10/20/30 across short periods); solve residual'
new_version = 'robert_lp__heuristic_ramp_high'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: A simple capacity rule: just slap 2 units of the biggest component into every single period and stop optimizing the timing. How much does that overbuild cost us versus the model's plan?

best = max(model.j, key=lambda j: value(model.c[j]))
for j in model.j:

manual.query: Why bother optimizing the dispatch hour by hour? Couldn't we just run both thermal units — coal and nuclear — flat out at their weekly capacity every hour and let the dam and the gap balance the rest?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('sddp_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic merit-blind policy: pin coal and nuclear to their full weekly capacity
# every hour (computed from wCapacity), leaving hydro/spill/reservoir/gap to balance.
HPW = 168
weeks = list(model.w)
def _week_of(t):
    return weeks[(int(t[1:]) - 1) // HPW]
for t in model.t:
    w = _week_of(t)
    model.X[t, 'HardCoal'].fix(value(model.wCapacity[w, 'HardCoal']))
    model.X[t, 'Nuclear'].fix(value(model.wCapacity[w, 'Nuclear']))
description = 'Heuristic: run coal and nuclear flat out at weekly capacity'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'sddp_lp__heur_thermal_flatout', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['sddp_lp__heur_thermal_flatout']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why over-think the routing? Couldn't we just run every factory flat out at capacity and split each one's output across the distribution centers in proportion to expected demand? Wouldn't that simple full-throttle rule hold a competitive profit?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('spbenders1_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic full-capacity policy: run every factory at capacity and split its output
# across the DCs in proportion to expected (probability-weighted) demand.
mean = {j: sum(value(model.scen_prob[s]) * value(model.scen_demand[s, j]) for s in model.S) for j in model.J}
total = sum(mean.values())
for i in model.I:
    for j in model.J:
        model.ship[i, j].fix(value(model.capacity[i]) * mean[j] / total)
description = 'Heuristic: full-capacity production, ship in proportion to expected demand'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'spbenders1_lp__heur_fullcap_propdemand', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['spbenders1_lp__heur_fullcap_propdemand']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Couldn't we keep it simple — just grow enough to cover expected demand at each depot and split each depot's supply across the farms in proportion to farm size? Wouldn't that demand-matching rule hold a competitive profit?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('spbenders2_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic demand-matching policy: serve each depot's expected (probability-weighted)
# demand, sourced from the farms in proportion to each farm's capacity.
mean = {j: sum(value(model.scen_prob[s]) * value(model.scen_demand[s, j]) for s in model.S) for j in model.J}
totcap = sum(value(model.capacity[i]) for i in model.I)
for j in model.J:
    for i in model.I:
        model.ship[i, j].fix(mean[j] * value(model.capacity[i]) / totcap)
description = 'Heuristic: serve expected demand, sourced in proportion to farm capacity'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'spbenders2_lp__heur_meandemand_capshare', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['spbenders2_lp__heur_meandemand_capshare']['obj']['value']))

# Heuristic front-load policy: buy the entire silo's worth of the best-yielding ingredient
# (highest yield into p-1) in the FIRST run only, nothing in later runs.
workhorse = max(model.i, key=lambda i: value(model.a['p-1', i]))
first = list(model.t)[0]
for i in model.i:
    for t in model.t:
        model.x[i, t].fix(value(model.b) if (i == workhorse and t == first) else 0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: front-load the entire silo with the best-yield ingredient in run one'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'srkandw_lp__heur_front_load', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['srkandw_lp__heur_front_load']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our matchmakers prefer to run the classic men-propose routine: each man asks down his own list, every woman just holds onto the best suitor she's gotten so far, and we let it settle. If we lock the matching to whatever that men-first process produces, how much worse is the total woman preference score than the optimizer's plan?
Why bother tuning a reorder schedule for every SKU — couldn't we just put everything on the minimum cadence and keep it dead simple? Wouldn't that be good enough?

Couldn't we just put every SKU on the same uniform cadence — say six reorders a period across the board — instead of fine-tuning each one? Wouldn't one standard schedule be easier to run and come out at least as cheap?

Our mission planners floated a dead-simple rule: just scan the swaths in numerical order — s0, then s1, then s2, all the way to s20, then close the loop back to s0 — and let the system only pick which node to enter and leave each swath. If we lock the visit sequence to that straight index order, how much longer is the total flight than the optimizer's clustered plan?

Our layout team likes a no-fuss rule of thumb: give every row the shortest height band whose widest cell still fits within an even per-column share of the page, and only go taller if nothing fits that share. If we lock the table to that simple rule instead of the optimizer's mix, how much taller does the table end up?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('tablelayout_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'shortest-row-within-an-even-budget': allow each column an equal share of the
# page width (pw / number of columns), then give each row the SHORTEST height band whose
# widest cell still fits that even share; if none fits, fall back to the tallest (narrowest)
# band. Fix the height picks and let the solver set the resulting cell/column widths.
budget = value(model.pw) / len(model.C)
heights_of_row = {r: sorted(h for (rr, h) in model.rh if rr == r) for r in model.R}
for r in model.R:
    pick = None
    for h in heights_of_row[r]:
        widest = max(value(model.minRCHw[r, h, c]) for c in model.C)
        if widest <= budget:
            pick = h
            break
    if pick is None:
        pick = heights_of_row[r][-1]
    for h in heights_of_row[r]:
        model.bRH[r, h].fix(1 if h == pick else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: shortest row height that fits an even per-column width budget'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'tablelayout_mip__heur', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['tablelayout_mip__heur']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why bother optimizing the food side at all? Couldn't the village just grow enough straight maize each year to cover the families' grain needs and skip the maize-after-tobacco rotation entirely? How much does that simple rule actually cost us?
manual.constraint:

# Heuristic: meet the village's domestic maize demand with direct maize cropping
# alone each year (area = total maize demand / maize yield) and drop the
# maize-after-tobacco rotation altogether.
nfam = value(model.nfam)
fdmaize = value(model.fdmaize)
yc_maize = value(model.tmd['yield', 'maize'])
maize_area = fdmaize * nfam / yc_maize
for t in model.t:
    model.x[t, 'maize'].fix(maize_area)
    model.mat[t].fix(0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: cover maize demand with direct cropping only, no maize-after-tobacco'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'tabora_lp__heur_maize_only', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(value(model.income)))

manual.query: Do we really need to optimize the whole thing? A cautious desk would just fill the first two lots and let the back half go. How much profit does that leave on the table?
Here's the compliance-first playbook: pass every lot but never let a class-3 high-risk pool into the deal. Lock that in and tell me what profit that conservative rule produces.

Why agonize over the investment timing? Couldn't we just build both mills on a flat, even schedule sized to the steady-state log volume the forest can deliver, and split it by how much capacity each mill draws per unit?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('tfordy_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic flat-build policy: replace optimized lumpy investment with an even capacity-build
# schedule. Size the annual build from the steady-state log throughput the forest can sustainably
# deliver: the existing-forest stock the mills draw on is capped by efs at
# sum_{s,k,u} iad[u,s]*scd[k]*land[s]/10 (1000 ha-equivalents); convert that at the average
# existing-forest yield yw to a total log volume, spread it evenly over the horizon, and split it
# between the two mills by their unit capacity-utilisation weight b.
nT = len(list(model.t))
yw_vals = [value(model.yw[te, u, s, cl]) for (te, u, s, cl) in model.yw if value(model.yw[te, u, s, cl]) > 0]
yw_mean = sum(yw_vals) / len(yw_vals)
cut_area = sum(value(model.iad[u, s]) * value(model.scd[k]) * value(model.land[s])
               for s in model.s for k in model.k for u in model.u) / 10.0
total_logs = cut_area * yw_mean
bw = {mm: sum(value(model.b[mm, p]) for p in model.p if (mm, p) in model.b) for mm in model.m}
tot_bw = sum(bw.values())
for mm in model.m:
    flat = (bw[mm] / tot_bw) * total_logs / nT
    for t in model.t:
        model.h[mm, t].fix(flat)
description = 'Heuristic: flat capacity build sized to steady-state log throughput, split by b'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'tfordy_lp__heur_flatbuild', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['tfordy_lp__heur_flatbuild']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Looking at the yield tables, pulplogs peak at age 20 across both species and across every site class — sawlogs don't really show up until much later rotations. # Heuristic planning strategy set in advance, then solve the residual:

If we lock Songkhla to its direct voyage v-04 that way, how much worse does the man-miles bill get versus the optimizer's mixed plan?

manual.query: Our dispatchers like a dead-simple rule: hand each market entirely to one plant, taking the markets biggest-first and giving each to whichever plant it fits into most snugly on remaining capacity. If we lock the network to that single-source pattern, how much worse is the freight bill than the optimizer's mixed plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('trnsindic_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'best-fit sole-source': handle markets largest-demand-first, assigning each
# entirely to the single plant whose remaining capacity fits it most tightly (smallest
# leftover slack), then let the solver set flow volumes on the fixed arc pattern.
rem = {i: value(model.a[i]) for i in model.i}
for j in sorted(model.j, key=lambda jj: -value(model.b[jj])):
    d = value(model.b[j])
    feasible = [i for i in model.i if rem[i] >= d]
    pick = min(feasible, key=lambda i: rem[i] - d)
    rem[pick] -= d
    for i in model.i:
        model.use[i, j].fix(1 if i == pick else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: best-fit sole-source market-to-plant assignment'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'trnsindic_mip__heur_bestfit', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['trnsindic_mip__heur_bestfit']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Forget the cost-juggling for a second — couldn't we just split each market's order between the two plants in proportion to how big each plant is? Seattle's the smaller cannery, San Diego the bigger, so every city draws from each in that capacity ratio. Wouldn't that simple fair-share rule hold a competitive shipping bill?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('trnsport_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic capacity fair-share policy: allocate each market's demand across the
# plants in proportion to each plant's capacity (a naive split that ignores cost geography).
total_cap = sum(value(model.a[i]) for i in model.i)
for j in model.j:
    for i in model.i:
        model.x[i, j].fix(value(model.b[j]) * value(model.a[i]) / total_cap)
description = 'Heuristic: capacity-proportional fair-share allocation across plants'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'trnsport_lp__heur_fair_share', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['trnsport_lp__heur_fair_share']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our dispatcher doesn't trust the fancy routing — he just runs the old greedy rule: start at the depot (city 1) and always drive to the nearest stop you haven't hit yet, then loop home at the end. If we lock the tour to that nearest-neighbor route, how much more does it cost than the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('tsp2_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'nearest neighbor': from the depot, repeatedly hop to the closest
# unvisited city, then close the loop back to the depot; fix x to that route.
cities = list(model.ii)
start = model.depot
unvisited = set(cities)
unvisited.discard(start)
cur = start
route = [start]
while unvisited:
    nxt = min(unvisited, key=lambda j: value(model.c[cur, j]))
    route.append(nxt)
    unvisited.discard(nxt)
    cur = nxt
seq = route + [start]
arcset = set((seq[t], seq[t + 1]) for t in range(len(seq) - 1))
for i in model.ii:
    for j in model.ii:
        if i == j:
            continue
        model.x[i, j].fix(1 if (i, j) in arcset else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: nearest-neighbor tour from the depot'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'tsp2_mip__heur_nn', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['tsp2_mip__heur_nn']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why fuss over the exact timing of nuclear? Couldn't we just take whatever total nuclear the plan wants and spread it evenly across the periods nuclear is allowed — 1993, 1998 and 2005? What does an even-build schedule like that cost us?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('turkpow_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic nuclear-timing rule: take the total nuclear capacity the optimal plan
# builds and spread it EVENLY across the three periods nuclear is permitted
# (1993, 1998, 2005), instead of letting the model optimize the timing.
tot = sum(value(model.ht['nuclear', t]) for t in model.t)
periods = [1993, 1998, 2005]
share = tot / len(periods)
for t in model.t:
    if t in periods:
        model.ht['nuclear', t].fix(share)
    else:
        model.ht['nuclear', t].fix(0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: even nuclear build across 1993/1998/2005'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'turkpow_lp__heur_nuclear_even', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['turkpow_lp__heur_nuclear_even']['obj']['value']))

    expr=sum(model.x[i, 'overtime', k, l] for i in model.i for k in model.k for l in model.l) <= 100.0
)

new_version = 'uimp_profit_lp__overtime_cap_100'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Looking at the revenue mix, one SKU is carrying way more than half of topline and it's a concentration risk the board is uncomfortable with. Shouldn't no single product clear more than 50% of total revenue, so we're not over-reliant on one line? What's keeping the model from picking a more balanced revenue mix?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('uimp_profit_lp', models_dictionary)
model.product_revenue_cap = ConstraintList()
for k in model.k:
    model.product_revenue_cap.add(
        sum(model.p[i, k] * model.z[i, k] for i in model.i)
        <= 0.5 * sum(model.p[i, kk] * model.z[i, kk] for i in model.i for kk in model.k)
    )
description = 'Why-not: cap each product at <= 50% of total revenue to reduce single-SKU concentration'
new_version = 'uimp_profit_lp__rev_concentration_cap_50'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Looking across the machines, m3 looks like it's barely pulling its weight while m1 and m2 do most of the work — the standing labor cost on m3 should buy us more of the line. Shouldn't each machine carry at least 30% of total production units, so we're getting the operator headcount we're paying for? What's making the model dump so little on m3?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('uimp_profit_lp', models_dictionary)
total_prod = sum(model.x[i, j, k, l] for i in model.i for j in model.j for k in model.k for l in model.l)
model.machine_load_floor = ConstraintList()
for l in model.l:
    model.machine_load_floor.add(
        sum(model.x[i, j, k, l] for i in model.i for j in model.j for k in model.k) >= 0.30 * total_prod
    )
description = 'Why-not: each machine must carry >= 30% of total production units for balanced floor utilization'
new_version = 'uimp_profit_lp__machine_load_floor_30'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Sales-ops is pushing a make-to-order discipline — set the plan up front to sell exactly the contracted demand in each season, no over-production and no inventory build, and let the solver settle the rest. Wouldn't that be simpler to run and roughly as strong on the topline? What's the model seeing that pulls it the other way?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('uimp_revenue_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a make-to-order plan set in advance — pin every season's sales to that
# season's contracted demand (no over-production, no inventory build), then solve the residual.
for i in model.i:
    for k in model.k:
        model.z[i, k].fix(value(model.d[i, k]))
new_version = 'uimp_revenue_lp__heur_make_to_order'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: pin sales of every product in every season to contracted demand exactly (make-to-order discipline); solve residual'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Why not just build out every plant and never worry about capacity — switch on all the ammonia and fertilizer sites and let shipping sort itself out? Wouldn't full coverage be worth it?

for j in model.jd:
    model.z[j].fix(1)
for i in model.id:
    model.w[i].fix(1)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: open every fertilizer and ammonia plant'
new_version = 'vietman_vietmip_mip__heur_open_all'

    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: I keep thinking we're overcomplicating this. Quarter 3 has the cheapest selling price ($8) and quarter 2 the dearest ($12) — so why not just run it as a plain buy-low, sell-high play: do all our purchasing in q-3, do all our selling in q-2, and leave q-1 and q-4 out of the trading entirely? Let the solver size the q-3 buy and the q-2 sell however it likes. Wouldn't that simple dedicated schedule be about as good as anything fancier?

Why pour money into kiln expansions at all — couldn't we just run the plant capacity we already have and cover the rest with imports? Wouldn't keeping the existing kilns and skipping all the new builds come out cheaper?
Big kilns feel risky to commit to. Couldn't we just keep things incremental and only ever build small kilns? Wouldn't a small-units-only program be good enough?
